Here's a quote for you from Dick Sutfen. And if you haven't paid attention to him, he's the interesting guy to go back and look at some of his old stuff. I got it exactly the way he said it. Mount Olympus is a gated community. Reluctance to declare yourself a god and constantly remind everyone of your status prohibits residents on Mount Olympus. That's pretty good, isn't it? I mean, I hated all this stuff early, by the way, personally, and I fought it for a long time. I don't like being in the self-aggrandizement business. It's not my favorite thing on the planet. But Halbert's quote is absolutely right. It is the business that we are in. I mean, ultimately, they're by and us, and they're believing in us more than they are believing in themselves. And so, to influence others, you've got to constantly tell them that they're supposed to be influenced by us. And as I said to somebody at the table, our world is not a world that it is usually useful to be subtle in. And so, I think it's important to recognize that that's really the business we're in. And not only do you have to do it to make a sale, but you have to do it constantly to remind them of your position with them and your status. And so, it now permeates into every piece of printed matter you ever put in front of them. And there's lots of ways to get at it. Testimonials are, of course, one of the best. Others telling everybody that we are God is, you know, at least as useful as us telling them in probably more useful in many ways. But it does not relieve us of the responsibility to say it. That's part and parcel of the plan. Now, you can do it sometimes sort of humorously, you know. I have this culturally ingrained, you know, that's why I get the big bucks. And in coaching meetings, we make sure we say it eight, ten, twelve times during the course of the coaching meeting. It's, you know, people now know it so they can say it in unison and so forth. But I mean, it's done for a reason, you know. And I have less humorous ways that I do it as well. It is a key element in every issue of the newsletter is reminding everybody of my status. Bill said yesterday, see most people equate our personal affluence with our position. And so you need to remind them of your affluence. Different people have different expressions of it, but just about everybody has some expression of it. So if you happen to have twelve classic cars and you're like a mini-lenno, but you live in a modest house by choice, by all means show off the cars. If you happen to live in the Taj Mahal place, by all means make a big point at the Taj Mahal place. If you like, you do a good job of it with the, you know, moved out into the middle of the woods with the Taj Mahal cabin that is four times over budget and the deer and the rabbits in the back yard. See, all that's right. But you can't just do it once and forget about it. You've got to keep rubbing their face in it. Hmm? What? Excuse the by the mansion deer. Yeah, it's an excuse to, excuse the by the mansion. Well, I mean, I mean, it's one of my excuses. It's one of my excuses for feeding all the horses. I mean, that's a way of showing affluence is really, and it's a way of showing autonomy. You know, I use it sort of for several purposes, but now I would do it anyway, by the way. So, you know, but I mean, if you happen to want to own a mansion, you're right. It's a nice way to, you know, to justify it. And the flip side is, don't not use whatever it is that you are peeing away money on, you know, by all means use it. But so this is, this is something that a lot of people are uncomfortable with and they under do it. For all sorts of reasons, childhood conditioning, religious programming on and on and on and on and on and on. But it's a mistake to under do it. And just about everybody who cranks it up profits by doing so. It is, you know, like Charlie, you and I have had this conversation. I mean, your sort of loss of control of them in a coaching environment is in part because you're not constantly smacking them over the head, you know, with your godlike status and your superhero superiority. You know. There's different tones. You can do that. You can have the eye of the all media versus like you sometimes, as you say, like in a joke, say, wow, I've got these 23 Ray forces to feed. So there's almost like a self deprecating. I'm kind of crazy to be spending this money. We speak for a minute about, you know, it's kind of like, why, my wife persuaded me to buy this 8,000 square foot house. She's always wanted the mansion, frankly. Yeah, I mean, I'm crazy, but it really doesn't matter to me. I mix tone. Okay. But I use pretty much all of them. The same approach you're describing, like if you heard Tommy Hopkins at the super conference, he does the same thing with the wife and the horses and the two houses and he like blames it on her. But the reason he's telling it is not to be entertaining and not to blame it on her. The reason he's telling it is to show the mental picture of the huge ranch and the horses and the plane. And that that's why he's telling the story, you know. And so like, think about like the way Hanson does it versus the way Cobain does it. Okay, there's stylistic differences. Cobain's a little more elegant about it. Hanson sort of, oh, look what I got and who I'm with kind of both work. But big mistake is not to do it at all. And and big mistake is not to do it a lot. And so yeah, I tend to use the couch it in humor more than other things. But if you look closely, you'll see at some points I ram it at him too. And I don't just mean the affluence. I mean the that's just one of the symbolic representations, if you will, of the being on Mount Olympus. You know, I mean, we're talking about telling them how brilliant you are, telling them that you possess secrets they don't have, telling them you have experience they don't have, telling them there's all these people standing in line to give you tons of money. And you know, not doing that is a mistake. This is why you have your console schedule and the newsletter. Yeah, I learned that from Cobain, by the way, when he was like doing nothing.
